:name is released under the MIT License,: :name is released under the MIT License, the whole source, no strings. Read it, fork it, ship it commercially, run it on your own hardware. No vendor lock-in, ever.

A collection can use many types; an item: A collection can use many types; an item picks exactly one.
A collection holds a set of items, give: A collection holds a set of items, give it a name and set who can see it.

A reset link will be sent if the account: A reset link will be sent if the account exists.
A series links related items into a broa: A series links related items into a broader franchise or body of work, like Harry Potter or Marvel, regardless of type or collection. Unlike sets, a series has no target and no completion.
A set is a checklist of the items that b: A set is a checklist of the items that belong together: a full run, a series, a want list. KolleK tracks which ones you own and how close you are to complete.
A set lives inside one collection and co: A set lives inside one collection and counts towards a target. A series does neither: it reaches across every collection in the account to gather everything from one franchise, whatever shape it takes.
A type cannot have more than :max fields: A type cannot have more than :max fields.
A type cannot have more than :max groups: A type cannot have more than :max groups.
API keys are like secret passwords that: API keys are like secret passwords that allow other tools or apps to connect securely to your account.

We are still writing the full story for: We are still writing the full story for this feature. In the meantime, browse the rest of what :name can do.
:name keeps an activity history of chang: :name keeps an activity history of changes across the account and on individual items. When something looks different, you can see who changed it and when. This is not an interrogation tool. Mostly.

<strong>:owner</strong> invited you to t: :owner invited you to the household collection as an :role.
A clear owner, editor, and viewer model: A clear owner, editor, and viewer model is enough for your household, club, or small team.
A collection can be a solo pursuit. It c: A collection can be a solo pursuit. It can also be a household project, a club archive, or the shared responsibility of people who all have strong opinions about where the rare one belongs.
A shared collection still needs a source: A shared collection still needs a source of truth.
Can add & edit. Can’t manage members.
Everyone can help. Not everyone needs th: Everyone can help. Not everyone needs the big red button.
For households, clubs, and the one perso: For households, clubs, and the one person who always edits the wrong field
Give everyone one place to look, one voc: Give everyone one place to look, one vocabulary to use, and the right amount of access. That tends to make “where did it go?” a less frequent conversation.
Owners manage the account. Editors add a: Owners manage the account. Editors add and update the collection. Viewers can browse without accidentally reclassifying the entire shelf. Clear enough to remember; useful enough to trust.
People with access to the Geller househo: People with access to the Geller household account.
Send an invitation, welcome someone into: Send an invitation, welcome someone into the account, and give them a role that matches how they contribute. No shared password written on a note. No “just be careful” permissions model.
Send an invitation. Not a shared passwor: Send an invitation. Not a shared password.
The feature status page has the boring-b: The feature status page has the boring-but-important details.

You only need a list of titles.
One row per thing is plenty.
You do not care which exact copy you own: You do not care which exact copy you own.

Each physical copy gets its own record.
Condition, location, value, and acquisit: Condition, location, value, and acquisition details matter per object.
Because two copies are rarely interchang: Because two copies are rarely interchangeable.
For when “I think I have two” is not a s: For when “I think I have two” is not a system
Yes, the duplicate is a different creatu: Yes, the duplicate is a different creature.
One catalogue entry can describe the tit: One catalogue entry can describe the title. The copies beneath it describe the real objects you own: the pristine one, the battered one, the graded one, and the one currently hiding somewhere extremely safe.
The title is not the whole story: The title is not the whole story
One clean record. Every real object unde: One clean record. Every real object underneath.
Keep one clean item record for the thing: Keep one clean item record for the thing itself, then track every physical copy underneath it. No duplicate item names. No mystery suffixes. No “final-final-actually” spreadsheets.
One row says you own three. It cannot te: One row says you own three. It cannot tell you which three, where they are, or what any single one is worth.
Give every copy its own facts: Give every copy its own facts
The first pressing and the scuffed spare: The first pressing and the scuffed spare are not the same job.
Condition, location, acquisition date, p: Condition, location, acquisition date, price paid, estimated value, and status all belong to the individual copy. Because a first pressing on display and a scuffed spare in a box are not doing the same job.
Choose a copy. The details follow the ob: Choose a copy. The details follow the object.
Choose a copy and :name changes the deta: Choose a copy and :name changes the details around it: where it lives, what it is worth, and what happened to it. The history follows the object, not the title.
Built for the things collectors actually: Built for the things collectors actually collect
Variants. Editions. Duplicates. Graded i: Variants. Editions. Duplicates. Graded items. The version you bought because the cover was different. They all deserve a record of their own.
You need global search across every coll: You need global search across every collection today.
One flat alphabetical list is your ideal: One flat alphabetical list is your ideal organization system.
You want focused in-collection filtering: You want focused in-collection filtering and several ways to browse what is already in front of you.
Categories, locations, sets, series, tag: Categories, locations, sets, series, tags, and conditions reflect how you think about the collection.
The shelf is not a database. Sadly.
Organize it your way. Then find it again: Organize it your way. Then find it again.
Collections grow. Shelves fill. The box: Collections grow. Shelves fill. The box labelled “other” becomes a lifestyle. :name gives you several sensible ways to arrange what you own, then lets you browse it according to the question in front of you.
Browse the way your brain works.
Use a visual grid when covers do the tal: Use a visual grid when covers do the talking, a list when you want to scan quickly, or a table when details need comparing. :name remembers the view you prefer for each collection.
Build locations from room to cabinet to: Build locations from room to cabinet to shelf. Use categories, tags, series, and conditions to describe the other ways an object belongs. One collection; more than one useful map.
Keep an eye on the missing pieces.
Sets make completion visible. You can se: Sets make completion visible. You can see what you have, what you are still chasing, and whether you have accidentally collected more than the target. It happens.
Filter the collection in front of you.
Narrow a collection by the items already: Narrow a collection by the items already loaded on the page. It is quick, focused, and useful when you know where to look.
Give the collection a shape you can actu: Give the collection a shape you can actually navigate.
Grid, list, and table views. Locations,: Grid, list, and table views. Locations, categories, sets, series, tags, and conditions. Filter what is in front of you and get on with it.
Value over time is built from the valuat: Value over time is built from the valuations you recorded, not a live market feed.
Acquisition trends come from each copy’s: Acquisition trends come from each copy’s earliest acquiring transaction date.
Every point traces back to a record, so: Every point traces back to a record, so you can check the maths when something looks off.
could use a better photo or note.
You need live market prices supplied aut: You need live market prices supplied automatically.
You want speculative estimates without r: You want speculative estimates without recording where they came from.
You want your own transactions and valua: You want your own transactions and valuations to become useful collection insights.
You want a clear distinction between wha: You want a clear distinction between what you paid and what a copy is worth now.
You want value over time, acquisition tr: You want value over time, acquisition trends, top items, location value, and completion progress.
Spreadsheets have had a good run: Spreadsheets have had a good run
Finally, numbers that know what they are: Finally, numbers that know what they are talking about.
Record what you paid and what each copy: Record what you paid and what each copy is worth today. :name turns that history into a collection view that answers useful questions without making up the missing bits.
Cost and value are two different numbers: Cost and value are two different numbers.
Transactions record money that actually: Transactions record money that actually changed hands. Valuations record what an object may be worth. Keep both, and the collection stops confusing cost with value. A surprisingly popular mix-up.
Valuations · what it may be worth: Valuations · what it may be worth
A copy’s current estimated value comes f: A copy’s current estimated value comes from its latest valuation. No valuation? :name calls it unvalued, not worthless. Those are very different things.
Value over time, and everything that sha: Value over time, and everything that shapes it.
Watch value over time, acquisitions by m: Watch value over time, acquisitions by month, top items, condition distribution, and value by location. The numbers come from records you entered, so you can trace them back when something looks odd.
A sparse chart is a nudge, not a scoldin: A sparse chart is a nudge, not a scolding.
Sparse charts are not a scolding. They a: Sparse charts are not a scolding. They are a useful nudge that a few older copies still need a date, a value, or a better story.
Records in. Insights out. Nothing invent: Records in. Insights out. Nothing invented in between.
Every figure on the dashboard points bac: Every figure on the dashboard points back to a transaction or valuation you entered. That is the whole trick.
Pull the source and the Compose stack th: Pull the source and the Compose stack that ships with it.
Copy the example env, then set your app: Copy the example env, then set your app key and database password.
Four containers start together: app, que: Four containers start together: app, queue, scheduler, and MySQL.
Migrations run on boot. Open the app in: Migrations run on boot. Open the app in your browser.
Serves the app over nginx and PHP-FPM, a: Serves the app over nginx and PHP-FPM, and runs database migrations on boot.
Processes background jobs on the high, d: Processes background jobs on the high, default, and low queues.
Runs the artisan schedule: recurring cle: Runs the artisan schedule: recurring cleanup and maintenance tasks.
Holds your collection, items, copies, an: Holds your collection, items, copies, and history: the source of truth.
Uploaded photos, documents, and generate: Uploaded photos, documents, and generated files. Local disk or any S3-compatible bucket.
Every item, collection, valuation, and h: Every item, collection, valuation, and history entry. A mysqldump or a volume snapshot both work.
Photos, documents, and attachments. Rest: Photos, documents, and attachments. Restoring a database without these leaves broken references.
:name encrypts data at rest, so without: :name encrypts data at rest, so without this key the encrypted values cannot be read back. Easy to forget, painful to lose.
You want a zero-maintenance hosted servi: You want a zero-maintenance hosted service and never want to see Docker.
You need a deployment platform to make e: You need a deployment platform to make every operational decision for you.
You want a documented, supported route t: You want a documented, supported route to running the full app on infrastructure you choose.
A small server, Docker Compose, and a cl: A small server, Docker Compose, and a clear operating model sound like good news.
Some assembly required. Not much, though: Some assembly required. Not much, though.
Your own collection app. On your own ter: Your own collection app. On your own terms.
:name is a full collection app you can r: :name is a full collection app you can run on infrastructure you choose. Start with Docker Compose, keep the moving parts understandable, and retain a clear path for upgrades and backups.
Four commands from an empty directory to: Four commands from an empty directory to a running instance. The last one is “docker compose up”, not a leap of faith.
A short route from nothing to running: A short route from nothing to running
Bring up the web app, queue worker, sche: Bring up the web app, queue worker, scheduler, database, and storage with a documented setup, instead of assembling a stack from forum posts and optimism.
Five parts. That is the whole cast.
:name uses a web role, a queue role, a s: :name uses a web role, a queue role, a scheduler role, MySQL, and durable storage. Each part has a job, and each part is described in the docs.
Your data volumes survive the swap.
Updates preserve the data volumes that h: Updates preserve the data volumes that hold the database and uploaded files. Follow the documented upgrade path, run the migrations, and keep moving.
Containers are replaced. The <strong>nam: Containers are replaced. The named volumes stay put, so the swap touches code, not your collection.
Backups are part of the deal: Backups are part of the deal
A reliable backup includes the database,: A reliable backup includes the database, uploaded files, and the application key. It is not glamorous. It is extremely worth doing.
No automated backup command ships yet. S: No automated backup command ships yet. Snapshot the two named volumes, keep your APP_KEY somewhere safe, and standard Docker tooling handles the rest.
One documented path covers install, upgr: One documented path covers install, upgrade, and backup. Start with Docker Compose and keep every moving part where you can see it.
A fixed, genre-specific database already: A fixed, genre-specific database already contains every field you need.
You never need to add a field, option, o: You never need to add a field, option, or grouping.
You want a massive public reference data: You want a massive public reference database maintained for you.
Your collection has its own vocabulary.
You want reusable types, custom fields,: You want reusable types, custom fields, and forms that make sense to your hobby.
For people who have ever said “it’s basi: For people who have ever said “it’s basically the same thing”
Your hobby has jargon. We came prepared.
A watch is not just a thing with a name. A comic is not just a book. A bottle is not just a drink. Start with a collection type that gets close, then teach :name the details that actually matter to you.
Start from a ready-made type, then make: Start from a ready-made type, then make it yours.
:name arrives with ready-made types for: :name arrives with ready-made types for comics, trading cards, vinyl, books, watches, wine, coins, video games, and more. Pick one, make it yours, and get on with the satisfying bit: cataloguing the collection.
The fields your hobby actually uses.
Issue number. Pressing. Publisher. Vinta: Issue number. Pressing. Publisher. Vintage. Signed? First edition? Where it came from? Add the fields you need, choose the right kind of answer, and stop making one generic notes field carry the emotional weight of your entire hobby.

Group it, order it, hide the rest.
Put related details together. Keep the i: Put related details together. Keep the important stuff near the top. Move the rest out of the way until you need it. It is your collection; the form should behave accordingly.
The same item page, speaking your hobby.
Same :name. Two types. Neither pretends: Same :name. Two types. Neither pretends to be the other.
Build a type once. Use it everywhere.
Build a type once and use it across the: Build a type once and use it across the account. A carefully tuned “Vinyl” type should not have to be rebuilt every time another shelf becomes a collection.
Pick a ready-made type, add the fields t: Pick a ready-made type, add the fields that matter, and catalogue your collection the way it deserves.
The same reference, bought years apart,: The same reference, bought years apart, carries different service, value, and provenance.
Each copy keeps its own receipts, apprai: Each copy keeps its own receipts, appraisals, and certificates.
Nothing gets averaged, merged, or quietl: Nothing gets averaged, merged, or quietly overwritten.
A current value and a single note are en: A current value and a single note are enough.
You do not need the old story.
You need legal chain-of-custody or speci: You need legal chain-of-custody or specialist appraisal software.
You want purchases, valuations, care, lo: You want purchases, valuations, care, loans, moves, and documents in one timeline.
You would like the receipt, the record,: You would like the receipt, the record, and the object to agree with each other.
Receipts have a habit of going walkabout: Receipts have a habit of going walkabout
Keep the good story. Lose the paper chas: Keep the good story. Lose the paper chase.
A physical object has a life beyond its: A physical object has a life beyond its title and cover photo. :name keeps the purchase, the latest valuation, the service, the loan, the move, and the paperwork together, where they belong: with the exact copy they describe.
A copy’s timeline turns separate records: A copy’s timeline turns separate records into one readable story. See what happened, when it happened, and why it mattered, without opening eight tabs or excavating a folder named “misc final final”.
The receipt lives with the record it pro: The receipt lives with the record it proves.
Attach a receipt to the purchase. Keep a: Attach a receipt to the purchase. Keep an appraisal beside the valuation. Store the certificate with the object it certifies. Future you will be quietly pleased.
Headline version or the whole saga: Headline version or the whole saga
Read the big moments, or every last corr: Read the big moments, or every last correction.
Use the meaningful timeline when you wan: Use the meaningful timeline when you want the big moments. Switch to the complete view when you need every move, service, and correction. Both are there; neither judges you for being thorough.
Turn a record type back on to see its ev: Turn a record type back on to see its events.
Two copies of the same watch. Two differ: Two copies of the same watch. Two different stories.
Two copies of the same watch can have co: Two copies of the same watch can have completely different stories. :name keeps them separate, because blending them would make both records worse.
Keep the purchase, the valuation, the se: Keep the purchase, the valuation, the service, the loan, the move, and the paperwork with the exact copy they describe.

Decrypts the encrypted fields. Without i: Decrypts the encrypted fields. Without it, the backup is gibberish too.
Pull a new image on your schedule, not o: Pull a new image on your schedule, not ours.
Real copies of the database, storage, an: Real copies of the database, storage, and key.
The application key never leaves your in: The application key never leaves your instance.
You do not want any responsibility for h: You do not want any responsibility for hosting, upgrades, or backups.
You need end-to-end encryption where the: You need end-to-end encryption where the operator cannot access application data.
You expect an automated in-app backup bu: You expect an automated in-app backup button.
You want to decide where the app, data,: You want to decide where the app, data, photos, and documents live.
You want sensitive fields encrypted at r: You want sensitive fields encrypted at rest and control over the encryption key.
You are willing to make real backups of: You are willing to make real backups of the database, storage, and application key.
Your collection. Your server. Your sligh: Your collection. Your server. Your slightly over-specified NAS.
Keep your collection where you can point: Keep your collection where you can point at it.
:name is built to run on infrastructure: :name is built to run on infrastructure you choose. Keep the app, your photos, your documents, and the details of what you own under your control.
Run :name yourself with Docker. Your dat: Run :name yourself with Docker. Your database and uploaded files live where you decide they live, not behind a vague promise and a login page you hope stays around forever.
Sensitive details are not stored as plai: Sensitive details are not stored as plain text.
:name encrypts sensitive fields at rest. Names, values, locations, and item details are protected in the database by your instance’s application key. Curious database thieves get gibberish.
Backups are real, not a decorative butto: Backups are real, not a decorative button.
A complete backup includes the database,: A complete backup includes the database, uploaded photos and documents, and your application key. Keep all three. Losing the key would be an extremely bad plot twist.
All three, together. Restore into any :n: All three, together. Restore into any :name instance and you are exactly where you left off.
You are in charge. That is the point.
Self-hosting asks you to own upgrades an: Self-hosting asks you to own upgrades and backups. In exchange, you get meaningful control over the collection and the system that holds it.
The operator holds the encryption key; b: The operator holds the encryption key; backups are an operator responsibility.

One tiny thumbnail per title is enough.
You need professional digital-asset-mana: You need professional digital-asset-management workflows with advanced image editing.
Covers, labels, packaging, and detail sh: Covers, labels, packaging, and detail shots help you recognize what you own.
You want one account-wide photo library: You want one account-wide photo library with search, filters, and bulk cleanup.
Because you recognize the cover before t: Because you recognize the cover before the catalogue number
A collection should look this good on sc: A collection should look this good on screen, too.
Covers, labels, packaging, and detail sh: Covers, labels, packaging, and detail shots are part of how collectors remember what they own. :name gives the visual side of a collection enough room to do its job.
More than one photo, when one photo is o: More than one photo, when one photo is obviously not enough.
Attach several images to an item, choose: Attach several images to an item, choose the cover that represents it best, and reorder the gallery until the first thing you see is the right thing.
Use the collection grid when the cover,: Use the collection grid when the cover, label, or box art is faster than the title. It is often faster. We are not going to pretend otherwise.
Every item photo also lives in an accoun: Every item photo also lives in an account-wide photo library. Search by file or item name, filter covers from extras, sort by size, and find the images that need a little attention.
The details are there when you need them: The details are there when you need them.
See file size, format, dimensions, uploa: See file size, format, dimensions, upload date, uploader, cover status, and the item a photo belongs to. Useful for the careful collector and the person wondering why storage disappeared.

You want a practical log of care, condit: You want a practical log of care, condition, custody, storage, and provenance.
For the objects that would make a bad da: For the objects that would make a bad day worse
Keep the important details close. Very c: Keep the important details close. Very close.
Some objects need more than a title and: Some objects need more than a title and a photo. :name keeps insurance, care, custody, condition, storage, and proof of ownership beside the physical copy they describe.
Sum insured tracks the <strong>latest ap: Sum insured tracks the latest appraisal below.
Record insurance details alongside the l: Record insurance details alongside the latest valuation and the documents that support it. When someone asks what it is worth, you will have more than a hopeful answer.
Log cleaning, servicing, restoration, an: Log cleaning, servicing, restoration, and repairs. Record the condition before and after. The object keeps its history; you do not have to keep it in your head.
Light spine stress and one corner tick. Un-slabbed and unverified.
Pressed and encapsulated. Stan Lee signa: Pressed and encapsulated. Stan Lee signature verified on the label.
Know where it went, and whether it came: Know where it went, and whether it came back.
Track loans and returns, including who h: Track loans and returns, including who has the object, when it left, and what condition it was in when it came home. Awkward conversations are not eliminated, but they are better documented.
While an outgoing loan is active, this c: While an outgoing loan is active, this copy is marked as not in your physical custody, so a quick glance tells you it is out on exhibition, not lost.
Build a credible record over time.
